records.ety: [OE. ese, eise, F. aise; akin to Pr. ais, aise, OIt. asio, It. agio; of uncertain origin; cf. L. ansa handle, occasion, opportunity. Cf. Agio, Disease.]

quotes: They him besought Of harbor and or ease as for hire penny.

defs: Freedom from anything that pains or troubles; as: (a) Relief from labor or effort; rest; quiet; relaxation; as, ease of body.
Freedom from care, solicitude, or anything that annoys or disquiets; tranquillity; peace; comfort; security; as, ease of mind.
Freedom from constraint, formality, difficulty, embarrassment, etc.; facility; liberty; naturalness; -- said of manner, style, etc.; as, ease of style, of behavior, of address.
quotes: Usefulness comes by labor, wit by ease.
Give yourself ease from the fatigue of watching.
Among these nations shalt thou find no ease.
Take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry.
True ease in writing comes from art, not chance.
Whate'er he did was done with so much ease, In him alone 't was natural to please.

records.ety: [OE. esen, eisen, OF. aisier. See Ease, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Eased (ēzd); p. pr. & vb. n. Easing.]

defs: To free from anything that pains, disquiets, or oppresses; to relieve from toil or care; to give rest, repose, or tranquillity to; -- often with of; as, to ease of pain; to ease the body or mind.
quotes: Eased [from] the putting off These troublesome disguises which we wear.
Sing, and I 'll ease thy shoulders of thy load.

defs: To render less painful or oppressive; to mitigate; to alleviate.
quotes: My couch shall ease my complaint.

defs: To release from pressure or restraint; to move gently; to lift slightly; to shift a little; as, to ease a bar or nut in machinery.
To entertain; to furnish with accommodations.
